cards.definition: a metal containing iron — e.g. mild steel, cast iron. Magnetic and prone to rust
a metal without iron — e.g. aluminium, copper, brass. Generally corrosion-resistant
a plastic that softens when heated and can be reshaped — e.g. acrylic, PET. Recyclable
a plastic that sets permanently when heated and cannot be remoulded — e.g. epoxy resin, urea formaldehyde
wood from deciduous (broad-leaved) trees — e.g. oak, beech, mahogany. Generally harder and more expensive
wood from coniferous (evergreen) trees — e.g. pine, spruce. Generally softer and cheaper
a material made from two or more materials combined — e.g. carbon fibre reinforced polymer (CFRP), fibreglass
a material that responds to environmental changes — e.g. shape memory alloy, photochromic pigment
the maximum stress a material can withstand when stretched before breaking
designing and making products with minimal environmental impact — using renewable resources, reducing waste

questions.question: What is a ferrous metal?
options: A) A metal without iron — resistant to rust
B) A metal containing iron — magnetic and prone to rust
C) A type of thermoplastic
D) A composite material
questions.explanation: Ferrous metals contain iron — they are magnetic and prone to rust (corrosion). Examples: mild steel, cast iron, stainless steel (iron + chromium).

questions.question: What is a thermoplastic?
options: A) A plastic that sets permanently when heated
B) A plastic that softens when heated and can be reshaped
C) A natural material
D) A metal alloy
questions.explanation: Thermoplastics can be heated and reformed repeatedly — making them recyclable. Examples: acrylic (PMMA), PET (drinks bottles), polyethylene, nylon.

questions.question: What is a thermosetting plastic?
options: A) A plastic that softens when heated
B) A plastic that sets permanently and cannot be remoulded
C) A natural polymer
D) A recyclable plastic
questions.explanation: Thermosets set permanently through a chemical reaction — they cannot be remelted or reshaped. Examples: epoxy resin, melamine formaldehyde, urea formaldehyde.

questions.question: What is the difference between hardwood and softwood?
options: A) Hardwood is always harder than softwood
B) Hardwood is from deciduous trees; softwood is from coniferous trees
C) Softwood is from deciduous trees; hardwood is from coniferous trees
D) There is no botanical difference
questions.explanation: Hardwood is from broad-leaved deciduous trees (oak, beech). Softwood is from cone-bearing coniferous trees (pine, spruce). Note: balsa is a hardwood but is physically very soft.

questions.question: What is a composite material?
options: A) A single pure material
B) A metal alloy
C) A material made from two or more materials combined
D) A naturally occurring material
questions.explanation: Composites combine materials to gain the best properties of each — e.g. carbon fibre + resin = CFRP (very strong, lightweight). GRP (fibreglass) is another example.

questions.question: What is a smart material?
options: A) A material that is the most expensive
B) A material that responds to environmental changes
C) A material that is always recycled
D) A natural composite material
questions.explanation: Smart materials: shape memory alloys (return to original shape when heated), photochromic materials (change colour in light), piezoelectric materials (generate electricity under pressure).

questions.question: What is tensile strength?
options: A) A material's resistance to compression
B) A material's flexibility
C) The maximum stress a material can withstand when stretched before breaking
D) A material's hardness
questions.explanation: Tensile strength measures how much pulling force a material can withstand — important for ropes, cables, and structural members under tension.

questions.question: Why are non-ferrous metals generally preferred for outdoor use?
options: A) They are magnetic
B) They are cheaper than ferrous metals
C) They are corrosion-resistant — they do not rust
D) They are stronger than ferrous metals
questions.explanation: Non-ferrous metals (aluminium, copper, brass) do not contain iron so they do not rust — making them suitable for outdoor or marine applications.

questions.question: What is sustainability in design and technology?
options: A) Using the most expensive materials
B) Designing with minimal environmental impact — using renewable resources, reducing waste
C) Only using natural materials
D) Making the most durable product possible
questions.explanation: Sustainable design considers the full lifecycle — material extraction, manufacture, use, and disposal. It aims to minimise environmental impact and use renewable resources.

questions.question: Which plastic is used to make most clear drinks bottles?
options: A) Acrylic
B) Urea formaldehyde
C) PET (polyethylene terephthalate)
D) Epoxy resin
questions.explanation: PET (polyethylene terephthalate) is a thermoplastic — lightweight, transparent, and widely recycled. Used for drinks bottles and food packaging.

questions.question: Ferrous metals contain iron and are prone to rust.
questions.explanation: Iron content makes ferrous metals magnetic and susceptible to corrosion (rust) when exposed to water and oxygen.

questions.question: Thermoplastics cannot be recycled.
questions.explanation: Thermoplastics can be REHEATED and reshaped — they ARE recyclable. Thermosetting plastics cannot be recycled.

questions.question: Hardwood always comes from physically hard trees.
questions.explanation: Hardwood comes from DECIDUOUS (broad-leaved) trees. Some hardwoods (balsa) are physically very soft.

questions.question: Composite materials combine two or more materials to achieve better properties.
questions.explanation: E.g. CFRP combines carbon fibre (strong) with resin (binds fibres) — achieving high strength and low weight.

questions.question: Thermosetting plastics can be remelted and reshaped.
questions.explanation: Thermosetting plastics set permanently — they cannot be remelted or remoulded.

questions.question: Smart materials respond to changes in their environment.
questions.explanation: Shape memory alloys, photochromic materials, and piezoelectric materials all respond to external stimuli.

questions.question: Non-ferrous metals are magnetic.
questions.explanation: Non-ferrous metals do NOT contain iron and are generally NOT magnetic.

questions.question: Tensile strength measures how much compression a material can withstand.
questions.explanation: Tensile strength measures resistance to STRETCHING (tension). Compressive strength measures resistance to being crushed.

questions.question: Softwood comes from coniferous (evergreen) trees.
questions.explanation: Softwood trees (pine, spruce, larch) are coniferous — they retain their needles year-round.

questions.question: Sustainable design considers the full lifecycle of a product.
questions.explanation: From material extraction to manufacture, use, and disposal — sustainable design minimises environmental impact at every stage.

questions.question: ________ metals contain iron — they are magnetic and prone to rust.
questions.explanation: Mild steel and cast iron are ferrous metals.

questions.question: ________ metals contain no iron — they are generally corrosion-resistant.
questions.explanation: Aluminium, copper, and brass are non-ferrous metals.

questions.question: ________ plastics soften when heated and can be reshaped — they are recyclable.
questions.explanation: Examples: acrylic, PET, polyethylene, nylon.

questions.question: ________ plastics set permanently and cannot be remoulded.
questions.explanation: Examples: epoxy resin, melamine formaldehyde, urea formaldehyde.

questions.question: ________ wood comes from deciduous (broad-leaved) trees — e.g. oak, beech.
questions.explanation: Hardwoods are generally more expensive and durable than softwoods.

questions.question: ________ wood comes from coniferous trees — e.g. pine, spruce.
questions.explanation: Softwoods are generally cheaper and easier to work with.

questions.question: A ________ material combines two or more materials — e.g. carbon fibre reinforced polymer.
questions.explanation: Composites gain the best properties of their component materials.

questions.question: A ________ material responds to environmental changes — e.g. shape memory alloy.
questions.explanation: Smart materials change properties in response to temperature, light, pressure, or other stimuli.

questions.question: ________ strength is the maximum force a material can withstand when stretched.
questions.explanation: High tensile strength is important for cables, ropes, and tension members.

questions.question: ________ design minimises environmental impact using renewable resources and reducing waste.
questions.explanation: Sustainable design considers the full lifecycle of a product.

questions.instruction: Match each term to its correct description.

pairs.match: contains iron — magnetic, prone to rust
softens when heated — can be reshaped — recyclable
two or more materials combined — e.g. CFRP

cards.definition: a statement of the problem to be solved — outlines the purpose and context of the design task
a detailed list of criteria the final product must meet — measurable and testable
designing for human use — considering size, comfort, ease of use, and safety
the measurement of the human body — data used to ensure products fit users
a working model made to test and evaluate a design before full production
using software to create 2D drawings or 3D models — e.g. AutoCAD, Fusion 360
using computer-controlled machines to manufacture products — e.g. CNC router, laser cutter, 3D printer
a design process involving repeated cycles of designing, testing, and improving
a set of components working together to perform a function — input, process, output
evaluating the environmental impact of a product through its entire lifecycle

questions.question: What is a design specification?
options: A) A rough sketch of an idea
B) A detailed, measurable list of criteria the product must meet
C) The final product
D) The design brief
questions.explanation: The design specification sets measurable criteria — size, weight, cost, materials, performance — against which the final product is evaluated.

questions.question: What is ergonomics?
options: A) The measurement of the human body
B) Designing for human use — considering comfort, size, and safety
C) Computer-aided design
D) A type of prototype
questions.explanation: Ergonomics ensures products fit and work well for human users — good ergonomic design reduces fatigue, injury, and user error.

questions.question: What is anthropometrics?
options: A) Designing for environmental impact
B) The measurement of the human body used in product design
C) A type of material testing
D) The lifecycle of a product
questions.explanation: Anthropometric data (e.g. average hand size, reach) ensures products are designed to fit the target user — avoiding exclusion of parts of the population.

questions.question: What is CAD?
options: A) Computer-Aided Delivery
B) Using computer software to create 2D drawings or 3D models
C) A type of prototype testing
D) A manufacturing process
questions.explanation: CAD software (AutoCAD, SolidWorks, Fusion 360) allows designers to create, modify, and test designs digitally — saving time and reducing errors.

questions.question: What is CAM?
options: A) Computer-Assisted Marketing
B) Using computer-controlled machines to manufacture products
C) A type of material testing
D) A design specification tool
questions.explanation: CAM machines (CNC routers, laser cutters, 3D printers) are controlled by CAD data — enabling accurate, repeatable manufacturing.

questions.question: What is iterative design?
options: A) Designing a product once and manufacturing it
B) A process of repeated cycles — design, test, improve
C) Manufacturing the same product repeatedly
D) Evaluating a finished product
questions.explanation: Iterative design: design → prototype → test → evaluate → improve → repeat. Each cycle refines the product.

questions.question: What is a design brief?
options: A) A list of measurable criteria
B) A statement of the problem to be solved — outlines purpose and context
C) A final design drawing
D) A prototype
questions.explanation: The design brief identifies the design problem, the target user, and the context — it gives the designer direction without specifying the solution.

questions.question: What is a prototype?
options: A) A final finished product
B) A working model to test and evaluate a design before full production
C) A computer model only
D) A design specification
questions.explanation: Prototypes are made to test and evaluate designs — they may be rough models (foam, card) or fully functional working models.

questions.question: What is lifecycle assessment (LCA)?
options: A) The lifespan of a product
B) Evaluating the environmental impact of a product across its entire lifecycle
C) Assessing the cost of a product
D) Testing the durability of a material
questions.explanation: LCA considers: raw material extraction → manufacturing → use → end of life (disposal/recycling). It helps designers minimise environmental impact at each stage.

questions.question: What is a system in design and technology?
options: A) A manufacturing process
B) A set of components working together — input, process, output
C) A design brief
D) A type of material
questions.explanation: Systems thinking: input (something that enters) → process (what happens) → output (what results). E.g. a circuit: battery (input) → switch (process) → light (output).

questions.question: A design specification should be measurable and testable.
questions.explanation: Specifications like 'must weigh less than 200g' are measurable — vague criteria like 'should be good' are not acceptable.

questions.question: Ergonomics considers the measurement of the human body.
questions.explanation: ANTHROPOMETRICS measures the human body. ERGONOMICS uses this data to design products that fit and work well for humans.

questions.question: CAD allows designers to create and modify designs digitally.
questions.explanation: CAD software enables 2D and 3D design, simulation, and modification — faster and cheaper than physical drawing.

questions.question: Iterative design involves designing a product once and going straight to manufacture.
questions.explanation: Iterative design involves repeated cycles of design → test → improve.

questions.question: A prototype tests a design before full-scale production.
questions.explanation: Prototypes allow designers to identify problems and refine designs before committing to expensive full production.

questions.question: CAM machines include CNC routers, laser cutters, and 3D printers.
questions.explanation: All of these are computer-controlled manufacturing machines.

questions.question: Lifecycle assessment only considers the manufacturing stage.
questions.explanation: LCA considers the ENTIRE lifecycle — from raw material extraction to disposal or recycling.

questions.question: Anthropometric data is used to ensure products fit the target users.
questions.explanation: Anthropometric data about body dimensions ensures products are appropriately sized for the target population.

questions.question: A design brief specifies exact measurements and materials.
questions.explanation: The design brief identifies the PROBLEM — the design specification gives exact, measurable criteria.

questions.question: Systems consist of input, process, and output.
questions.explanation: All systems can be described in terms of what goes in, what happens, and what comes out.

questions.question: A design ________ is a statement of the problem to be solved.
questions.explanation: The design brief gives direction without specifying the solution.

questions.question: A design ________ is a detailed, measurable list of criteria the product must meet.
questions.explanation: Specifications are testable — e.g. 'must cost less than £10'.

questions.question: ________ designs products for comfortable, safe human use.
questions.explanation: Ergonomics reduces fatigue, injury, and error in product use.

questions.question: ________ is the measurement of the human body used in product design.
questions.explanation: Anthropometric data ensures products fit the target user population.

questions.question: ________ uses computer software to create 2D and 3D design models.
questions.explanation: CAD allows rapid modification and testing without physical prototypes.

questions.question: ________ uses computer-controlled machines to manufacture products.
questions.explanation: CNC, laser cutters, and 3D printers are all CAM machines.

questions.question: ________ design involves repeated cycles of designing, testing, and improving.
questions.explanation: Each cycle refines the product — getting closer to the ideal solution.

questions.question: A ________ is a working model made to test a design before full production.
questions.explanation: Prototypes may be rough models or fully functional working examples.

questions.question: ________ assessment evaluates a product's environmental impact across its entire life.
questions.explanation: LCA covers extraction, manufacture, use, and disposal.

questions.question: A ________ consists of input, process, and output components.
questions.explanation: Systems thinking applies to electrical, mechanical, and product design.

questions.instruction: Match each term to its correct description.

pairs.match: statement of problem — purpose and context
measurable criteria the product must meet
designing for comfortable, safe human use
